title: When will Clairemont park upgrade finally be completed?
author: U T Readers
raw_excerpt: The community is right to wonder what the city is doing.
body_text:
The city’s handling of its project to improve Clairemont’s Olive Grove Park, which has been underway for nearly two years, has left the community puzzled. Daily, complaints arise at the park and in the neighborhood about the project’s delay. Key areas remain unfinished, including field landscaping and replacing rusted baseball backstops. Although some sod was recently laid, the rest of the park is so neglected that weeds are already overtaking it. Will the city finally take action to complete this long-overdue project?

— Trevor Elkins, Clairemont

title: Q&A: Nicole Crosby, San Diego City Council District 2 candidate
author: Nicole Crosby
raw_excerpt: District 2 ranges from Clairemont to Point Loma in central and west San Diego.
body_text:
1. What is your view of how elected leaders won approval of first-ever trash collection fees on more than 200,000 single-family homeowners?

The trash fee is emblematic of the frustrations our community experiences with local government. My neighbors expect high-quality city services, from roads to our first responders, and they understand that this costs money. Trust is broken because the city wasn’t honest about the fee rate. People don’t see the promised enhanced results. Our streets still need paving, our streetlights are still out, and our budget isn’t staffing necessary positions that provide critical community services. Due diligence review, oversight and transparency cannot be delegated. The taxpayers deserve better results, and I’ll make sure they get them.

2. What is your view of the imposition of parking fees at Balboa Park and do you think the mayor and  council  did enough to control costs before taking this step?

Balboa Park is a place where every San Diegan has special memories and where families can enjoy low-cost fun. It is unfair to charge any fee to San Diegans before the city looks at how it spends money, handles contracts and manages land leases. The city’s failure to look inward, along with a lack of community input, shows how we ended up in this situation. Instead of quick fixes, we need to address the root cause of the problem to control costs and clearly explain the plan to impacted San Diegans.

3. Voters gave landslide approval in 2006 to a “managed competition” process in which some city services can be outsourced and strongly supported pension reforms for most city workers in 2012, but elected leaders have pursued neither policy in recent years. Would you?

City workers have always delivered strong, reliable service to the people of San Diego. “Managed competition” (privatization) didn’t deliver the savings or efficiency it promised. What it did do was take up a lot of time and resources from city staff. The real problem isn’t our hardworking employees, it’s poor budgeting decisions and management mistakes that hurt efficiency.

A new hire’s pension in 2026 is very different from one in 2005. Proposition B in 2012 ended up being a major pension problem for the city, both financially and in keeping employees happy and staying on the job. Pensions are predictable costs the city can plan for. Workers pay into them, and the city doesn’t have to pay Social Security, which saves money every year compared to private businesses. The city needs to make sure it pays what it owes.

As your next city c ouncilmember  for District 2, I will work with my colleagues to make sure city services are run efficiently by city workers and that we pay our bills on time.

4. What is the single issue you feel deserves more attention?

As a working mom, I know how hard it is for my family and my neighbors to keep up with growing costs for everyday necessities such as utility bills, trash and parking fees, insurance and rent. Throughout this campaign, I have heard from my neighbors about the trust they have lost in our local government, the need for infrastructure repair and concerns about public safety.

San Diegans must be treated like our basic needs matter. It is the everyday San Diegans that make this America’s Finest City. It matters that our streets need to be paved, that our streetlights are still out, and that the city isn’t staffing the necessary positions that provide critical community services.

As the next city c ouncilmember I will do the work. First, I will work to rebuild our community’s trust in local government by auditing our city budget, meeting with local stakeholders and putting a stop to passing policies in City Hall that haven’t been properly vetted by the neighborhoods they are impacting. Next, I will work alongside our county and state partners to ensure execution of a one-year, five-year and 10-year roadmap that will deliver world-class streets service and infrastructure to our communities. Finally, I will make sure that our fire and police departments are fully staffed to ensure fire preparedness and address emergency response times.

Go to nicoleforsandiego.com for more information about the candidate.

title: Q&A: Jacob Mitchell, San Diego City Council District 2 candidate
author: Jacob Mitchell
raw_excerpt: District 2 ranges from Clairemont to Point Loma in central and west San Diego.
body_text:
1. What is your view of how elected leaders won approval of first-ever trash collection fees on more than 200,000 single-family homeowners?

I oppose prioritizing fees before fiscal responsibility. Residents are charged while the city continues approving deficit budgets.

2. What is your view of the imposition of parking fees at Balboa Park and do you think the mayor and council did enough to control costs before taking this step?

I oppose and am personally offended by Balboa Park parking fees. The city has allowed program scope creep, middle manager bloat and approved deficit budgets, but it turns to user fees instead of fixing spending. Balboa Park is core public infrastructure, not a luxury. Charging for access limits foot traffic in one of our most important cultural areas and hurts working families. I would vote to reverse these fees. If revenue is needed, it must come after demonstrated spending reform. Public access should not be the city’s budget lever.

3. Voters gave landslide approval in 2006 to a “managed competition” process in which some city services can be outsourced and strongly supported pension reforms for most city workers in 2012, but elected leaders have pursued neither policy in recent years. Would you?

Managed competition is a useful tool; if a service can be delivered at equal or better quality for less, we need to use it. However, the larger issue is pensions, consuming 15%-20% of the General Fund yearly. Past fixes, like 2012’s Proposition B, failed in court due to improper negotiations.

We cannot just cut our way out of pension liabilities, regardless of what anyone says. The only path forward is pragmatic, legally durable reform. I would reduce middle manager bloat, and negotiate directly with labor for longer vesting periods, hybrid plans and adjusted benefits for new employees, thereby reducing long-term liability without breaking existing commitments.

4. What is the single issue you feel deserves more attention?

Housing affordability needs far more attention. My view is pragmatic. I want to solve the problem by studying how the system actually behaves. San Diego currently has a housing system whose affordability intentions don’t align with outcomes. We want affordability. In practice, we have high fees that push projects toward the luxury market, long timelines, difficult approvals and legal complexity that advantages institutional developers and squeezes out everyone else (4705 Point Loma Avenue, Midway).

Our system does not reward modest, neighborhood-compatible home ownership opportunities. It rewards the players with enough capital, attorneys and patience to survive years of process, with developments that will leave communities in a rental wasteland. “Complete Communities” my A#%.

Right now, zoning has become overlegalized, and the city can use that complexity to ignore neighborhood character while still claiming it’s pursuing affordability. The result is often neither preservation nor affordability. It is just expensive, litigation-prone development that leaves us regular residents behind.

We should learn from cities that have successfully reopened the market to smaller-scale housing. Austin’s HOME reforms were designed to allow more housing types in single-family areas that increase supply without changing the community’s character with giant apartment complexes. Ones that mom and pop can develop.

San Diego should take that lesson and adapt it to our own neighborhoods. I would direct city engineers and planning staff to create 10 to 20 preapproved duplex, triplex and small multi-unit designs that fit the character of different communities. These should be attractive, practical and scaled for infill. If a resident chooses one of those plans and the lot qualifies, the project should move through an accelerated approval path.

That would do three things at once. It would cut timeline risk. It would lower soft costs. And it would let ordinary property owners help close the housing gap instead of making us dependent on billionaire-backed mega projects.

I want more room for the missing middle: homes that teachers, young families, and longtime San Diegans can actually imagine owning or building. We need a housing strategy that expands supply without flattening the individuality of our neighborhoods. That means fewer red carpets for giant developers and more pathways for residents to build the next generation of San Diego themselves.

Go to jacobmitchell2026.com for more information about the candidate.

title: Q&A: Mandy Havlik, San Diego City Council District 2 candidate
author: Mandy Havlik
raw_excerpt: District 2 ranges from Clairemont to Point Loma in central and west San Diego.
body_text:
1. What is your view of how elected leaders won approval of first-ever trash collection fees on more than 200,000 single-family homeowners?

Trash collection is a basic city service, and residents deserve a system that is fair, transparent and easy to understand.

Right now, that’s not what we’re seeing. The rollout has been confusing, with unclear communication and a transition process that left many residents frustrated. The city should have done a much better job preparing people for this change.

I’m especially concerned about how this fee is being collected through the county property tax bill. Requiring the full amount upfront creates real risks, particularly for seniors and those on fixed incomes. No one should be put in a position where falling behind on a basic service could lead to liens or even foreclosure.

This needs to be corrected. Any fee should be clearly explained, fairly structured and limited to covering actual costs, not generating excess revenue.

Residents deserve accountability, transparency, and a system that works for them, not against them.

2. What is your view of the imposition of parking fees at Balboa Park and do you think the mayor and council did enough to control costs before taking this step?

I strongly oppose parking fees at Balboa Park. This is our city’s crown jewel, and it should remain accessible to everyone. Charging for parking moves us toward a system of haves and have-nots, where access to public spaces depends on your ability to pay. That’s not the kind of city we should be building.

The same concern applies to our beaches. Public access should remain open and equitable. Instead of charging residents more, the city should focus on better cost controls and explore alternative revenue sources that don’t burden everyday San Diegans.

3. Voters gave landslide approval in 2006 to a “managed competition” process in which some city services can be outsourced and strongly supported pension reforms for most city workers in 2012, but elected leaders have pursued neither policy in recent years. Would you?

Voters supported these policies because they wanted efficiency and accountability. Those goals still matter, but this shouldn’t be about ideology, it should be about results.

I’m open to managed competition when it clearly improves service delivery and reduces costs without compromising quality or harming workers. At the same time, we need to invest in our city workforce and make sure departments have the tools they need to succeed. Every decision should be focused on delivering better outcomes for residents.

4. What is the single issue you feel deserves more attention? 

Affordability, full stop. Not just housing, but the full cost of living. It’s your SDG&E bill, your gas bill, your water bill, your groceries and now new city fees layered on top.

People are being squeezed from every direction. We need a city that actually works for us. That means every decision at City Hall should be grounded in one question: How does this impact the people who live here?

We need better budgeting practices, sticking to approved spending, prioritizing essential services and using taxpayer dollars wisely. Before asking residents to pay more, the city needs to show it is managing what it already has responsibly.

At the same time, we should invest in solutions that help people stay in their homes, rent relief for older adults, emergency prevention funding and targeted support for those on the edge of displacement.

Residents should see value in their city. If we’re asking people to contribute more, they should feel it in safer streets, reliable services and stronger neighborhoods. That’s how we restore trust and make sure San Diego works for the people who call it home.

San Diegans are being asked to pay more at a time when the cost of living is already pushing people to the brink. What’s missing from too many decisions at City Hall is clarity, fairness and follow-through.

We need to rebuild trust by being honest about costs, improving how we budget and delivering real value to residents. We should look beyond residents for revenue by maximizing our Transient Occupancy Tax and ensuring online travel companies pay their fair share. Visitors and corporations should be part of the solution, not just residents.

Go to mandyhavlik.com  for more information about the candidate.

title: Q&A: Josh Coyne, San Diego City Council District 2 candidate
author: Josh Coyne
raw_excerpt: District 2 ranges from Clairemont to Point Loma in central and west San Diego.
body_text:
1. What is your view of how elected leaders won approval of first-ever trash collection fees on more than 200,000 single-family homeowners?

City trash collection has never been free and has been underfunded for decades, with many properties receiving city service when they shouldn’t. Only 18 cents of every dollar of property tax goes to the city to fund essential services like police, fire, lifeguards, as well as waste and recycling collection — the math simply doesn’t work long term. Sadly, this was an opportunity for the city to get it right — and it missed the mark. By speeding up the process to meet the constraints of last year’s budget, our residents had to experience delays getting new bins, struggled to reach customer service with questions, and were left navigating a confusing transition alone. Now residents face multiple service providers operating on different days, using three different cans, staying on the curb five nights a week. After just receiving new trash, recycling and organic waste bins, many residents were asked to switch again. That’s not just inefficient — it’s frustrating.

2. What is your view of the imposition of parking fees at Balboa Park and do you think the mayor and council did enough to control costs before taking this step?

On day one, I will call for a vote to immediately suspend the new citywide parking package and go back to the drawing board for a new plan. This isn’t just resistance to change — it’s a plan that isn’t working. This policy was hastily implemented all in the name of doing the work to avoid difficult budget cuts. From Petco Park to Balboa Park, the parking reforms are harmful, inconsistent, confusing and difficult to navigate.

Residents are not renewing their museum membership because of the added cost and frustration. A thoughtful, transparent plan that works for the entire region and supports our cultural assets takes more than just two months. But instead, we are now burdening San Diegans with a system that is extracting minimal return and could be costing the city more. Fines and fees will not solve the budget deficit.

3. Voters gave landslide approval in 2006 to a “managed competition” process in which some city services can be outsourced and strongly supported pension reforms for most city workers in 2012, but elected leaders have pursued neither policy in recent years. Would you?

Managed competition has had two decades for proponents to show viability and cost savings — and it hasn’t worked. The latest example is the city’s outsourcing of ambulance services. We watched the provider fail to meet basic standards that residents demanded and the city forced them to work with the company they had just replaced — costing the city time, money and endangering lives.

Public service is a noble calling, and we should not blame city workers, or the pension, for the budget problem. The courts overturned the attempt to “reform” the pension and ended up costing taxpayers millions more. We should always look for ways to deliver better results for residents. Our most reliable answer is the city workforce having tools and resources they need to deliver quality results for the taxpayers.

That means measuring performance, holding departments accountable, and being transparent about what is and is not working. My focus is simple. Honor commitments to the public, support workers and make sure City Hall delivers reliable, high-quality services that San Diegans expect.

4. What is the single issue you feel deserves more attention?

San Diego is expensive and everyone can see the explosion in the cost of living. Groceries cost more, utility bills are higher, gas is expensive, child care is out of reach for families, and every day the chaos of the Trump administration makes a recession seem right around the corner. The basics are not keeping up. Roads need repairs, streetlights are out, services feel inconsistent and I see the results of our homeless crisis every day.

I believe San Diego’s best days are still ahead of us, but only if we are willing to fight for them. That means getting serious about affordability for our residents, cutting the constant nickel-and-diming by creating stable funding, and delivering a city that works. No more excuses. It’s time to start delivering.

Go to electjoshcoyne.com  for more information about the candidate.

title: Cutting city’s arts funding would be a profound error
author: U T Readers
raw_excerpt: The mayor's cuts would have a devastating effect.
body_text:
Re “San Diego arts leaders blast proposed massive funding cuts as ‘catastrophic’ and ‘devastating’” ( April 15 ): At a time when cities compete to attract talent and tourism, San Diego is considering cuts that would move us in the wrong direction. The proposed cuts to arts and culture funding would erode institutions that drive our economy and keep our city vibrant.

The arts are not expendable — they educate, connect and define us. From student programs to performances that unite communities, arts organizations are central to San Diego’s civic and cultural life.

For organizations like San Diego Opera, these cuts would be devastating. The Opera employs 21 full-time staff, nine teaching artists, and over 350 artists and craftspeople each season. Its $11 million annual impact is part of an arts sector generating $1.2 billion. These cuts threaten jobs, businesses and San Diego’s cultural identity.

I urge city leaders to reconsider.

— Ross Russell, San Diego

Young people need arts education. It’s not a frill. At least 77 organizations in San Diego provide arts education in music, visual arts, dance, media and performing arts. These programs provide residencies in schools and community centers, after-school programs, learning onsite at cultural institutions and more. Many also provide social services or internships. Thousands of young people benefit from these often free or low-cost programs that ignite learning and spark originality.

Exposure to museums, live performances and visual arts connects youngsters to the cultural community and cultivates future creatives. Studies report that students involved in sustained arts participation show increases in test scores, grade point averages and graduation rates, as well as gains in self-confidence, empathy and compassion.

Reducing funding for the many arts and cultural organizations that serve our children will have a profoundly devastating effect across all grade levels and communities.

— Judy Berman Silbert, Rancho Peñasquitos

title: O Sea, a popular seafood spot in Orange, to close this month
author: Brock Keeling
raw_excerpt: A Costa Mesa-based restaurant group has purchased the Old Towne eatery and its intellectual properties.
body_text:
Opening in 2021 in Old Towne Orange , O Sea will close its doors for good, with its final service slated for Thursday, April 30.

King’s Seafood Company, a Costa Mesa-based restaurant group, acquired the O Sea brand and its intellectual property. O Sea founder Mike Flynn will move into a senior role at the company as vice president of culinary and brand development, while the restaurant’s general manager Sarah Everett will take on a leadership training and development role within the organization.

“This is a proud and full-circle moment,” said Sam King, founder of King’s Seafood Company, in a written release. “Mike began his career with us early on, and it’s incredibly meaningful to welcome him back in a leadership role. His creativity, perspective, and commitment to quality are in complete alignment with our company’s values and will be invaluable as we continue to evolve our brands and invest in the future of our organization.”

ALSO READ: Old Towne Orange has nearly 50 restaurants. How did it become a dining destination?

O Sea staff will be offered the chance to interview for jobs within King’s Seafood Company’s portfolio of restaurants, which include Water Grill, King’s Fish House, 555 East Steakhouse, Lou and Mickey’s, Meat on Ocean and Pier Burger.

“I’m incredibly proud of what our team built with O Sea,” said Flynn in the same release. “Joining King’s Seafood Company is an exciting next chapter and an opportunity to bring our ideas and experience into a broader platform. It’s an honor to rejoin an organization that played such an important role in my early career.”

Flynn opened the seafood spot (the name a playful riff on “OC”) on Glassell Street in June of 2021. It built a following for such inventive dishes as Oaxacan hot shrimp and ahi crispy rice alongside its adherence to sustainable practices.

“My deep passion for responsible coastal cuisine led me to develop this concept, which has been designed to make seafood approachable, contemporary and just plain fun for our guests,” Flynn said in a 2021 “Orange County Register” interview .

There are no plans to reopen O Sea at another location. It remains unclear what will replace the Glassell Street space once O Sea shutters.

title: John Seiler: Education reformer Gloria Romero aims for lieutenant governor
author: John Seiler
raw_excerpt: Romero gives Republicans, and real reform, their best chance in two decades.
body_text:
It was a delight talking to Gloria Romero, whose education reform work I’ve backed for two decades. Now she is running for lieutenant governor of California in the June 2 primary. The former Democratic majority leader in the state Senate became a Republican in Sept. 2024 and backed Donald Trump for president. 

She’s teaming with Republican gubernatorial candidate Steve Hilton on the Golden Ticket. While working with Hilton on policy papers at his Golden Together nonprofit, he said to her, “Look, I’m going to run for governor,” and asked her to be his running mate. Romero joked, “Steve, we really don’t have running mates in California.” Common practice for both major parties is to run separate campaigns for the two positions.

But soon they realized, she said, “This would be really good, because it sends the message that the governor and the lieutenant governor really should be working in tandem, like the president and the vice president of the United States.”

I interviewed Hilton for my column almost a year ago. An immigrant from the United Kingdom, he was a Fox News host before becoming an entrepreneur in Silicon Valley. Romero’s policy background would be crucial for his success as both a candidate and governor. That experience would include knowing the difficulty of getting anything done in a Legislature dominated by the public employee unions, especially the California Teachers Association. 

Romero earned her B.A. and M.A. from Cal State Long Beach, and her Ph.D. in psychology from UC Riverside. She has been a professor at Cal State LA and other universities. She especially has advocated for charter schools, including co-founding Explore Prime Academy in Orange . That background is crucial because K-12 spending is 40% of the state budget. And parents are most concerned about their kids’ education.

Her signature legislation in the Senate was the “parent trigger” law. Officially it was Senate Bill X5-4 from 2010, the Parent Empowerment Act. It allowed a majority of parents to vote to restructure school management. Unfortunately, she said, “The unelected state Board of Education rewrote the rules and neutered it. Although it’s still on the books. That’s what happens. The empire strikes back.”

Which shows how valuable Romero would be to Hilton in advancing real education reform, which she said is needed more than ever. I brought up how Gov. Gavin Newsom’s budget proposal for 2026-27 slates average school spending at $27,418 per pupil. He boasted that’s a 60% increase in his seven years in office. Yet school scores remain low.

Romero mentioned reforms in Mississippi. The New York Times reported in January, “Mississippi has gone from 49th in the country on national tests in 2013, to  a top 10 state  for fourth graders learning to read — even as test scores have  fallen almost everywhere else .”

She said since she left the Senate 16 years ago, and while her reform efforts were thwarted, “Mississippi has bypassed us.” She also pointed out the lieutenant governor can directly push for education reform as a voting regent at the UC system, a trustee at the Cal State system and a member of the Board of Governors of the California Community Colleges.

Alas, for Republicans the biggest hurdle remains voter registration at 45% Democratic, 25% Republican, 23% No Party Preference and 7% other parties or unknown. That’s as of Dec. 30, 2025, per the Secretary of State’s   tally . 

Related Articles Agustina Vergara Cid: Young Americans need more clarity about energy Susan Shelley: Why the Legislature wants to kill ‘greenwashing’ lawsuits Jon Coupal: Electric vehicle mandates costing government and taxpayers With Orbán’s defeat, MAGA needs new foreign despot to admire Rafael Perez: The eternal return of Mike Schaefer, candidate for Congress “We want to put together a new governing coalition for California, starting with the Republican base and pulling in disaffected Democratic voters,” Romero said. Town halls with Hilton across the state have shown support for reform. Even in Democratic Los Angeles, “The overwhelming expression was, because of regulations, businesses are shutting down, especially in Hollywood with the film industry.”

Hilton’s endorsement by President Trump pushed him to first place at 18% in a Kreate Strategies poll released April 20. The problem: for the Nov. 3 general election, facing a Democrat will engage the full force of opposition from the public employee unions.

But Romero gives Republicans, and real reform, their best chance in two decades.

John Seiler is on the SCNG Editorial Board

title: The illusion of choice: Why California’s top-two primary must go
author: Mimi Robson
raw_excerpt: The courts may say it passes constitutional muster; that does not mean it is serving the voters it is supposed to represent.
body_text:
California’s top-two primary system has once again survived a legal challenge. But while the courts continue to uphold it, the more important question is whether it actually serves voters. From where I stand, it does not.

In a ruling issued April 13, U.S. District Judge Maxine M. Chesney dismissed most of the claims brought by several minor parties, including the Libertarian Party of California. The decision leaned on familiar ground, concluding that the Constitution does not require the state to create a system that produces diverse outcomes or gives smaller parties a meaningful path to the general election. That may settle the legal question, but it does not settle the practical one.

The top-two system is often defended as neutral. All candidates appear on the same primary ballot, and the top-two vote-getters advance. On paper, that sounds fair. In practice, it consistently narrows the field in a way that limits voter choice, not expands it. In California, this problem is magnified by political reality. The state is, for the most part, dominated by one party. As a result, it is not uncommon for the general election ballot to feature two candidates from that same party. When that happens, a large portion of voters are effectively left without a real choice in November. That outcome is not hypothetical; it is routine.

What makes this more troubling is when those decisions are being made. The primary election, where the field is actually reduced to two candidates, draws far fewer voters than the general election. In non-presidential years especially, participation drops off significantly. Looking at registered voters alone understates the issue. When measured against the total population eligible to vote, primary turnout in California often falls to between 15 percent and 20 percent. By contrast, midterm general elections typically see about 35 percent to 45 percent of eligible voters casting a ballot.

That gap is not minor. It is the difference between a small, less representative slice of the electorate deciding which candidates advance, and a much larger group of voters being left to choose from whatever remains. By the time most voters are engaged, the decision has already been made for them. Supporters of the top-two system argue that voters are free to participate in the primary and that the rules apply equally to everyone. That is true as far as it goes, but it ignores a basic reality: many voters do not closely follow primary elections, and turnout data reflects that. When participation is that low, the system effectively allows a minority of voters to determine the choices available to everyone else.

Efforts are now underway to pursue ballot initiatives that would replace the top-two system and return California to partisan primaries. The goal is straightforward: ensure that voters in November are presented with a wider range of choices that better reflect the diversity of political views in the state.

If the system consistently produces elections where many voters feel they have no real options, it is worth asking whether it is working as intended. The courts may say it passes constitutional muster; that does not mean it is serving the voters it is supposed to represent.

Mimi Robson is past chair of the Libertarian Party of California

title: Tax realities — Nuclear weapons and our priorities | Your Turn
raw_excerpt: Our national budget was funded on tax day, April 15. This is a time to reflect on our priorities and who we are as a nation. Each of us can identify funding priorities in our collective daily experience from childcare and education to healthcare, national defense, and even nuclear weapons. With finite dollars and a myriad of local, national and international needs, we must be informed as to how these funds are being allocated.
body_text:
Our national budget was funded on tax day, April 15. This is a time to reflect on our priorities and who we are as a nation. Each of us can identify funding priorities in our collective daily experience from childcare and education to healthcare, national defense, and even nuclear weapons. With finite dollars and a myriad of local, national and international needs, we must be informed as to how these funds are being allocated. 

Promises of affordability, reduced cost of living, and avoidance of costly wars have not coincided with reality. Here in Ventura County we see rising inflation, infrastructure needs, ongoing needs in addressing our houseless population and climate mitigation needs among others. 

Our planet continues to warm with progressive climate change causing increasing scarcity of natural resources further promoting conflict around the planet. Coupled with the potential for future global pandemics, this is a time when global cooperation and collaboration is more important than ever. 

Unfortunately, we are pursuing policies of increased isolationism, feigning international cooperation with disdain for the international rule of law. We have seen five of the nine nuclear armed nations at war this past year with China increasing rhetorical threats against Taiwan. The twin interconnected existential threats of climate change and nuclear war seem ever closer as noted by the Bulletin of Atomic Scientists who moved their symbolic Doomsday Clock to 85 seconds to midnight this past January. This is a grim reminder of our increased reliance on luck to prevent a nuclear catastrophe, either by intent, miscalculation or disruptive technology. The very existence of nuclear weapons threatens all of us, and everything we hold near and dear, every moment of every day.

The tax day release of the U.S. Nuclear Weapons Community Cost Project, now in its 37th year, finds the U.S. spending over $137 billion on all nuclear weapons programs.  That equates to an average of $401.51 for every man, woman and child based on an average income of $44,673 robbing our communities of precious resources that could be redirected to the actual needs of our citizens.

In California we are spending over $17.51 billion with Ventura County spending $371.49 million, the cities of Ventura $49.36 million, Oxnard $58.47 million and Ojai $4.13 million. An average California family of four will spend $1,780 dollars on nuclear weapons programs this year, and as a nation we are spending $261,092 every minute on weapons that cannot and must not ever be used without threatening all of humanity. 

Where does this fit into your priorities as you think about your family and the future you envision? 

This is a situation that does not have to be, but one that will not change without public support and outcry. A growing national grassroots campaign called Back from the Brink is bringing communities together to prevent nuclear war. The movement calls for the U.S. to take a leadership role in actively pursuing a verifiable agreement among nuclear-armed states to eliminate their nuclear arsenals with four precautionary measures to prevent nuclear war until abolition is verifiably achieved.

Over 250 local elected officials across the nation just sent an open letter to members of Congress urging bold action to reduce nuclear risks and work to eliminate nuclear weapons. Local Back from the Brink activists have reached out to all of our local officials. Thus far over 60 California elected officials joined, including: California Assemblyman Steve Bennett, Camarillo Vice Mayor  Dr. Martita Martinez-Bravo, Oxnard Mayor Pro Tem Gabriel Teran, Karen Sher, Board President Oxnard Union High School District and Ventura City Councilwoman Luz Campos. In addition to thanking our local leaders who have endorsed the campaign and encouraging others to join this effort, all citizens can endorse this campaign as well.

We all have a role to play in pursuing a future for our children and future generations. That role is unique to us and not necessarily a large role or a small role, it is our role. If our luck holds out, when our children’s children ask, what did you do when the planet was threatened, how will you respond? Working together we can make nuclear weapons a threat of the past.

Robert Dodge, M.D., is a retired family physician practicing living in Ojai. He serves on the board of Physicians for Social Responsibility Los Angeles and serves as the Co-Chair of the Committee to Abolish Nuclear Weapons of National Physicians for Social Responsibility. He is also serves on the Back from the Brink steering committee.

title: The 100-year rule of California reform | Mathews
author: email" href="mailto:?subject=The%20100-year%20rule%20of%20California%20reform%
raw_excerpt: Don’t worry, Californians, about obvious flaws in your state’s governing system. Our leaders will fix them.
body_text:
Don’t worry, Californians, about obvious flaws in your state’s governing system. Our leaders will fix them.

Just give them a century or so.

California’s economy reliably moves at world-class speed, but its government often lags 100 years behind. This is the state that didn’t ratify the 14th Amendment, added to the U.S. Constitution in 1868, until 1959.

So, in 2020s California, we are fixing 1920s problems.

In 2024, we removed ecosystem-destroying dams that were added to the Klamath River 100 years ago. After the pandemic the state updated early-20th-century health codes on infectious diseases. 2020s housing reform laws are overturning exclusionary zoning, local control, and apartment building bans from the Roaring Twenties.

But the best example of 1920s-inspired policymaking is playing out this year in the state education system.

As he leaves office, Gov. Gavin Newsom is advocating a structural reform first suggested in 1920. That year, a legislative committee identified the problem: California had two different entities in charge of education across the state.

One was the elected state superintendent of public instruction, which was established in the state constitution. The other was a governor-appointed State Board of Education, a regulatory body that added duties from ordering textbooks to setting graduation standards importance.

The 1920 report warned of political conflict between the board and superintendent — and contradictory guidance for school districts: “The present California educational organization must be regarded as dangerous for the future, and it should be superseded at the earliest opportunity by a more rational  form of state educational  organization.”

The report ’s “more rational” solution was to abolish the elected superintendent position and consolidate educational oversight under the State Board. The board could appoint a “commissioner of education” to take charge of education policymaking in the state.

Was this simple idea enacted?

Not yet.

Eliminating the elected superintendent’s position required a constitutional amendment. But voters, insisting that they wanted to elect superintendents, rejected amendments on four different ballots, in 1928, 1934, 1958, and 1968.

Over the last 30 years, six studies have urged major changes to California’s education governance model, according to the nonpartisan Legislative Analyst’s Office . All criticized the fact that no one person or institution could be held responsible for state education policy failures.

“The problem with California’s K‑12 governance system,” Governor Schwarzenegger’s committee on educational excellence found in 2007, “is that everyone is in charge, and no one is accountable.”

That still hasn’t changed. Indeed, in recent decades, the State Board of Education and the elected school superintendent have given school districts contradictory orders on English as a second language programs, local finance plans, ethnic studies instruction, and charter school rules.

So, this year, Newsom is taking another run at enacting that 1920 idea, via a proposed statute.

The governor’s plan is different in two ways. First, he is not proposing a constitutional amendment that would require voter approval. Second, he would shift power over education to the governor’s office. A new, governor-appointed education commissioner would manage the Department of Education. The elected superintendent would become another member of the State Board of Education, which would oversee the new commissioner. This is more complicated than eliminating the superintendent position entirely.  But it would put one official clearly in charge of education: the governor, acting through the new education commissioner.

“Adopting the new governance structure would allow for voters to hold the Governor accountable for change, reduce confusion about who is in charge, and improve oversight of existing programs,” the state’s non-partisan legislative analyst wrote recently.

So, is this one of those once-in-100-years moments?

Tony Thurmond, the current state superintendent, opposes the proposal. The powerful California Teachers Association, which gets friendly state superintendents elected, is not on board. Its opposition could doom the proposal.

So, don’t hold your breath. This could take another century.

Joe Mathews writes the Connecting California column for Zócalo Public Square .

title: Women’s health is not a niche issue — stop treating it like one
raw_excerpt: Preparing to welcome a new baby into the world is an extraordinary experience. This time is filled with excitement, anticipation, and hope for the future. But as I get closer to giving birth, I also find myself carrying concerns, not only for myself, but for my child.
body_text:
Preparing to welcome a new baby into the world is an extraordinary experience. This time is filled with excitement, anticipation, and hope for the future. But as I get closer to giving birth, I also find myself carrying concerns, not only for myself, but for my child. 

As a woman, I’ve faced too many challenges in the health care system: dismissals, misdiagnoses, and moments when my symptoms were minimized or overlooked. Those experiences stay with you, and I can’t help but wonder if I welcome a baby girl in a few months, will she face the same challenges?

That question becomes even more troubling when you consider that women make up nearly 51% of the population in the United States, yet our health has historically been understudied and overlooked. Women were even routinely excluded from clinical trials up until 1993. Because of this, much of modern medicine has been built on research conducted primarily on men, leaving critical gaps in understanding how diseases, medications, and treatments affect women’s bodies. 

And still today, women remain underrepresented in clinical research, with participation in some major trials hovering around 40%. These disparities are  even more pronounced  for women of color, who are significantly underrepresented in medical studies. 

When research fails to reflect the diversity of the population it aims to serve, it leaves doctors with incomplete information and patients with fewer answers, treatments and cures — something I know all too well. 

I was born with severe von Willebrand disease (VWD), a genetic bleeding disorder that affects the body’s ability to clot blood properly. It is actually the most common inherited bleeding disorder, but it often goes undiagnosed — especially in women.

The reason for these misdiagnoses is frustrating: many VWD symptoms overlap with experiences that society has long normalized for women. Heavy menstrual bleeding, frequent bruising, or prolonged bleeding after medical procedures are often dismissed as typical or expected. As a result, many women with bleeding disorders go years — sometimes decades — without a diagnosis.

Research shows women frequently report negative or dismissive interactions in health care settings, a pattern sometimes referred to as “medical gaslighting,” where symptoms are minimized or attributed to stress or other non-medical causes. These experiences can delay diagnoses, leading to prolonged suffering and worse health outcomes.

While VWD affects both men and women, women often experience additional complications and increased risks during pregnancy and childbirth if the condition is not properly managed.

Imagine if I hadn’t received my VWD diagnosis before I gave birth. Even if everything about my delivery went smoothly, I would have gone home unaware of the risks and potentially faced serious complications like a delayed postpartum hemorrhage. In the absolute worst-case scenario, that could mean not being there to watch my child grow up.

Instead, I’m grateful to have the knowledge and compassionate care needed to manage my condition effectively. Because I know that VWD is genetic, I can watch for symptoms and advocate for my child if needed, especially if I welcome a baby girl who might face the same diagnostic challenges that so many women do. Women’s health — particularly conditions that have long been misunderstood — deserves greater visibility, research, and respect.

Living with VWD as well as Crohn’s disease have given me the inspiration needed to not only advocate for myself, but for my loved ones, community members, and even people I haven’t yet met. I’ve traveled across the country speaking with lawmakers about policies that support people with rare and chronic conditions. Now, as I prepare to become a mother, these conversations feel even more personal and urgent.

Women of color in the U.S. face significantly higher risks of pregnancy-related complications and maternal mortality compared with white women. As a Latina, I am acutely aware of the disparities my community faces — from barriers to care to higher rates of pregnancy-related health concerns.

Over the past decade, patient advocates, researchers, and health care leaders have pushed for greater representation of women in clinical trials and more attention to conditions that disproportionately affect women. These efforts are beginning to close the knowledge gap — but there is still a long way to go.

I look forward to the day when young girls grow up knowing that severe bleeding is not normal, knowing they can advocate for themselves as soon as they find their voice. The day when future mothers don’t have to wonder whether the health care system will take them seriously.

Women make up more than half the population. It’s long past time that we receive equal representation in research — and equal respect when seeking care.

Mia Castañeda-Layman lives with von Willebrand disease and Crohn’s disease. Based out of Ventura County, she is a patient advocate and Vice President of the board for the  Bleeding Disorders Council of California .

title: Ventura County's arts ecosystem deserves our attention | Your Turn
author: email" href="mailto:?
raw_excerpt: When we think about what makes a community thrive, we often point to schools, health care, infrastructure and jobs. But there's another force quietly shaping the health and vitality of Ventura County: our arts and culture sector.
body_text:
When we think about what makes a community thrive, we often point to schools, health care, infrastructure and jobs. But there's another force quietly shaping the health and vitality of Ventura County: our arts and culture sector.

Arts and culture aren't luxuries reserved for special occasions. They're essential threads woven into the fabric of our daily lives, from the murals and street art that transform our cityscapes into sources of pride, to the concerts that bring neighbors together across differences, to the theatre productions that help young people find their voice.

Ventura County's creative industries generate more than $4.5 billion in economic impact, according to the most recent Economic Development Collaborative report. But the numbers tell only a part of the story. Arts organizations employ county residents, provide educational programming to thousands of students and draw audiences who support local businesses. Public art installations activate spaces that were once overlooked. But the real impact runs deeper than economics.

Arts and culture create conditions for communities to heal, connect and grow stronger together. When someone attends a dance performance exploring themes of immigration and belonging, they're not just being entertained; they're building empathy. When a teenager joins a youth theatre program, they're not just learning lines; they're developing confidence and collaboration skills that will serve them in any career. When a community gathers for a cultural festival celebrating their heritage, they're not just having fun; they're affirming their identity and strengthening social bonds.

This is what we mean by resiliency. The arts give us tools to process trauma, bridge divides, imagine new possibilities and remember what we're fighting for when times get hard.

Yet our arts ecosystem remains fragile. Artists struggle to afford studio space. Small arts organizations operate on shoestring budgets with skeleton staffs. Cultural programming that serves diverse communities competes for limited funding against commercially viable work.

The question isn't whether Ventura County has artistic talent; we do, in abundance. The question is whether we're creating the conditions for that talent to stay, grow and contribute to our shared prosperity.

Building a thriving arts sector requires intentional investment and collaboration. It means ensuring artists have affordable workspace. It means arts organizations have sustainable revenue models beyond constant fundraising. It means recognizing cultural programming as community infrastructure. It means ensuring arts reflect the full diversity of our county. It means paying artists fair wages and treating artistic work as legitimate work.

It also means the arts community must organize, advocate and support one another. Artists working in isolation can't shift policy or change funding priorities. But artists, cultural leaders and arts professionals working together, sharing knowledge, coordinating efforts and speaking with one voice, can reshape how our county values and invests in culture.

This is precisely why convenings like ReFrameVC Arts Summit matter. The one-day convening is designed to strengthen the region’s creative economy and support the people who power it. The third annual event will be Sunday, April 12, from 9:30 a.m.-5:30 p.m. at Studio Channel Islands Art Center (SCIArt) in Camarillo.

When we bring together the people who power Ventura County's arts sector, from painters and performers to gallery directors and arts administrators, we're not just networking. We're building the connective tissue of an ecosystem. We're identifying shared challenges and collaborative solutions. We're learning from one another's successes and failures. We're making the invisible infrastructure of the arts visible.

The workshops on innovative funding models help organizations become more sustainable. The panels on mental health and healing position arts as essential community well-being infrastructure. The focus groups feeding into statewide advocacy ensure Ventura County's arts priorities are represented in Sacramento. The connections made over lunch and happy hour become future collaborations that strengthen our entire region.

Ventura County stands at a crossroads. We can continue treating arts and culture as niceto-have amenities that get funded when budgets allow. Or we can recognize them as essential drivers of the healthy, connected, economically vibrant communities we all want to live in.

The arts ecosystem is already here, doing the work. The question is whether we'll invest in its success, because when culture thrives, we all benefit.

Niki Chopra Richardson is the Executive Director of TOArts and a Certified Fundraising Executive (CFRE) with over 20 years of nonprofit leadership experience, including an MBA from Pepperdine.

title: Music between sightseeing highlights | Woodburn
author: email" href="mailto:?subject=Music%20between%20sightseeing%20highlights%20%7C%20
raw_excerpt: “The music is not in the notes,” Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart is widely attributed to have noted, “but in the silence between.”
body_text:
“The music is not in the notes,” Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart is widely attributed to have noted, “but in the silence between.”

Similarly, the magic of travel is sometimes surprisingly found between landmark sites and famous sights.

So it was on a recent trip My Much Better Half and I took to New Zealand and Australia. The magic began in the airport, in a terminal restaurant, in a booth next to a father having dinner with two of the most adorable children imaginable — a daughter perhaps age 5 and a son surely not yet 1.

This family of three was just a delight to watch; the father held the boy lovingly in one arm as he ate with the other; the daughter, sitting across the table, had the manners and charm to match the princess-like dress she was wearing; when her brother, wearing a bow tie on this obviously special occasion, began to fidget, she made him giggle with an elixir of dancing facial expressions and a voice on the edge being a song.

Many, many years ago, on a trip when my daughter and son were not much older than this pair, a sweet stranger secretively paid the restaurant tab for us. Now, alas, the server said their bill had already been settled. Instead, I could only offer the father a compliment on his beautiful family.

And here came the real magic, for he said, nodding at the boy: “We just got him today.”

Was this an adoption homecoming trip? I did not ask, but I knew this: Our trip Down Under had just begun with our lips turned up into smiles.

A bookend airport scene filled with love occurred two days later, for we skipped a full date in flight, upon our arrival in Auckland.

As MMBH and I crossed the threshold into the arrivals reception area, a man I guessed to be in his 50s raced ahead of us into the open arms of a similarly aged man eagerly awaiting him. It was a vision out of a movie, complete with a long embrace that lifted one of the two off his feet; an embrace that went on and on; an embrace that was accompanied by wet eyes.

It was, I surmised initially, a lovers’ embrace. Or, perhaps, two long-lost old friends reunited? No and no — siblings, it turned out, for as I walked by I overheard one call the other, “Little Brother.”

My new smile widened further when I imagined, half a century from now, the young girl from the LAX restaurant running through an airport to happily and tearfully embrace her little brother.

Of the many unexpected sounds of music between the notes on this trip, here is one more.

New Zealand is renowned for its wines, and so MMBH and I toured a handful of wineries. The smallest one, off the beaten path, proved to be our favorite.

Its wonderful nectars, however, were not the reason.

With the seating all taken, we found an open spot against a wall to stand and sample two flights. Before either of us had finished our first small pour, a young woman walked across the room to invite us to join her party of four at their table.

“Party,” literally, because these friendly Kiwis — two sisters, one brother, and a husband — were celebrating a 30th birthday. The birthday boy kid brother, naturally, was the playful target of much laughter, and it was a joy to be included in this special occasion.

Indeed, singing “Happy Birthday” was more unexpected music on this trip.

Woody Woodburn writes a weekly column for The Star and can be contacted at  WoodyWriter@gmail.com . His books are available at  www.WoodyWoodburn.com .

title: Sorry, America, that we Californians count votes slow | Mathews
body_text:
1…2..3…4…5…

6…7…8…9…um… what’s next?

Oh, that’s right. 10.

Thank you for waiting, America, while this Californian took his time counting. I know that you get impatient when we Californians cou nt slowly, especially when counting votes.

Just think: With the time you wasted waiting for me just now, you could have slapped tariffs on allies. Or launched another war.

But I just had to get to 10. Because my fellow Californians and I want to take full responsibility for how long it takes us to count all our ballots.

So, let us say now, from the deep, dark bottoms of our hearts: We’re sorry, America.

We’re sorry that that we can’t give you final results on election night, that we require weeks to complete vote tallies. We’re sorry that we make you wait before you can threaten newly elected officeholders on social media.

We’re doubly sorry that we let our voters wait until Election Day to mail their ballots, giving them as much time as possible to consider their decisions and incorporate last-minute revelations. We are sorry that we don’t understand the virtues of just going with our guts on ballot choices — because careful deliberation is for sissies and Scandinavians .

Also, sorry that we have to give those ballots seven days after Election Day to arrive, because of the mess your government has made of the U.S. Post Office.

That might sound like enough apologies, but it isn’t. Sorry for limiting our sorries to contemporary processes. Real accountability means saying sorry for our history of expanding the franchise!

The New York Times, in an editorial , called out Californians for this grievous sin of letting too many people vote. In past centuries, the Times scolded, “Americans quickly learned the results of major elections. By the late 1800s, telegraphs made it possible for people to read results in the next day’s newspapers.”

Great point. We must apologize to the Times and to America for advancing beyond late-19th-century elections.

Among our grievous errors was granting voting rights to California women in 1911, which produced many more ballots to count. Apologies, too, for allowing our people to decide laws and change constitutions through direct democracy, through measures that make our ballots so long (and tedious to count). Sometimes our ballots have 50 or more contests.

Sorry for overdoing democracy!

We’re also sorry for California’s population growth since the 19th century, to nearly 40 million. If we hadn’t grown into the world’s fourth largest economy, we could run tiny, discriminatory elections like we did a century ago, and keep you and the Times happy.

Our bad!

But our apologies to America must go beyond history. We should say sorry as well for our recent campaigns to convince more Californians to register to vote.

I am sorry to report that California has increased its number of registered voters from 15.7 million in 2006 to over 23 million now . That more-than-7-million-voter increase is extraordinary because the state population only increased by 3 million in the same period.

Shame on us.

And sorry that all those new voters have created so many concerns about voter fraud among good-faith democracy advocates like President Trump and the Supreme Court.

And sorry that it’s easier to find proof that Justices Sam Alito and Justice Clarence Thomas secretly accepted gifts from billionaires than it is to find any real problems with our counts.

As a final note of contrition, we express our sorrow at your misunderstanding of our state. California, sorry to say, has all kinds of governance problems — in taxes, pensions, regulation, structure, and democratic participation. All could use more scrutiny.

Which is why we’re sorry, America, that you’re focused on our elections — a real California strength.

And we’re sorry that you can’t see it that way.

Joe Mathews writes the Connecting California column for Zócalo Public Square and is founder-publisher of Democracy Local .

title: Palm Springs shouldn't back off on airport expansion
raw_excerpt: “Palm Springs Airport delays massive expansion, unveils smaller changes.”
body_text:
“Palm Springs Airport delays massive expansion, unveils smaller changes.”

The above headline, published Tuesday, April 7, by The Desert Sun , certainly caught my attention. I had to read both this title and the article that followed twice to believe what I was reading and try to make sense of it. I could not.

The initial plan called for a $2.2 billion expansion consisting, among other projects, of 14 additional gates, two large rental car centers and expansion of the concourse.

Well, so much for the grand plan. My hopes for a modern terminal ready for the future deflated in a hurry. Seems like it is urgent to wait. The vision, with a capital V, shrank to what appears to be a cosmetic touchup.

The list of short-term improvements cited now is regrettably modest and looks more like ordinary maintenance work than anything else: restroom renovation, replacement of the escalators, relocation of the parking lot and upgrade of the departure baggage facilities.

What a disappointment! What a missed opportunity!

When my wife and I moved to Palm Desert a few years ago, as I was still traveling for my work between Silicon Valley and the desert, I was hoping that PSP would soon add new bridges to and from state and national business hubs. This would facilitate travel and attract new businesses and industries looking to expand in this magical area where people like to live. It would also create new and better local jobs, bring more money to the valley, increase property values and, in so doing, bring financial sustainability to the Coachella Valley.

Today, whether we recognize it or not, the future of the desert is not as bright as we would like to believe. Retail is hurting. Restaurants and hotels live dangerously. Businesses come or go. Stores and shops open or close. Aside from the medical industry and related businesses, others are suffering and the local jobs don’t pay much.

The prospect of an expanded regional airport was exciting for all kind of reasons, including the most important of all: The future, growth and economic success of a regional market start with a necessary airport expansion, one adapted to the future. This is step one.

The present thinking seems to be: Let’s wait until more people and money come to town, we will then go ahead with the big expansion plans. But it does not work that way. Growth does not happen by miracles. Growth is function of the major infrastructure improvements that are meant to invite it and make it happen.

Indeed, the economic growth of any significant regional market very rarely precede the construction of an airport open to national and international routes. Growth follows it. Number one comes first and acts as a magnet.

I often travel to Asia and the Middle East (Emirates). What we see there today is rather striking keeping in mind the object of this opinion piece. Huge stylish and ultramodern airports are mushrooming across the land to prepare for and adapt to the needs of new populations, businesses and tourist hubs 25 to 50 years down the road.

We are not there and probably will never be. We don’t need or want to be a metropolis. We just want to keep our desert to be a wonderful place to live, find a good job, raise a family and look forward to a bright future. The future starts with a real and meaningful expansion of our airport.

Alain Pinel lives in Palm Desert.

title: Gas leaf blowers are illegal in Palm Springs. So stop using them
raw_excerpt: In July 2017 the Palm Springs City Council voted to prohibit the use of gas-powered leaf blowers in the city — a ban that went into effect in 2019 .
body_text:
In July 2017 the Palm Springs City Council voted to prohibit the use of gas-powered leaf blowers in the city — a ban that went into effect in 2019 .

Residents were concerned about the noise from the gas-powered blowers and the chemical pollutants in the exhaust. Pollutants cause negative personal health issues and add to climate change in the long term. Noise has a more immediate adverse effect on residents, visitors and landscapers every day.

Some homeowners may not be aware of their responsibility to ensure their gardeners are complying with the ordinance, but homeowners can be fined if Code Enforcement observes (either from a complaint or proactively while out in the field) their gardeners using gas-powered blowers. A first violation carries a fine not exceeding $100, and fines can go up to $500 per violation if there are more than two violations within one year.

Rebates on electric blowers and batteries are still available from the South Coast Air Quality Management District (up to $250) and the city’s Office of Sustainability (up to $750 for a leaf blower and additional batteries).

To encourage compliance, please alert your gardener to these rebates and allow them to recharge their electric batteries onsite if needed.

In the six years since the ordinance took effect, the effects of climate change have become more pervasive and respiratory issues like asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) have increased, yet many landscapers continue to use gas-powered blowers.

Kathleen Cohn and Michael Frick are members of the Palm Springs Sustainability Commission.

title: COD, other California community colleges should offer 4-year degrees
raw_excerpt: For generations, California’s community colleges have stood as the front door to opportunity. They are where dreams begin — where working families, first-generation students, veterans and caregivers take the first step toward something more.
body_text:
For generations, California’s community colleges have stood as the front door to opportunity. They are where dreams begin — where working families, first-generation students, veterans and caregivers take the first step toward something more.

At College of the Desert, we are guided by four commitments: access, basic needs, enrollment and engagement. These are not just strategic pillars. They are a promise to our community.

But today, we face a hard truth: For too many students, the door we open does not lead all the way through.

The world has changed. Workforce expectations have changed. The question now is whether our system is willing to change with it.

Access has always been our strength. We meet students where they are, remove barriers, and open doors. But in today’s economy, access cannot stop at the associate degree. In fields like healthcare, advanced manufacturing, public safety, and technology, a bachelor’s degree is no longer a luxury — it is the baseline for advancement.

Each year, thousands of students, here and at other California community colleges, complete the coursework required to transfer to a four-year school. But the vast majority — typically over 90% — don't do so .

When students can begin with us but cannot finish — especially when geography, cost, or life circumstances prevent transfer — we have not fully delivered on the promise of access. True access means the ability to start and finish in the same place, supported by the same community that made success possible.

Our students are balancing real lives. They are parents, employees, and caregivers. We have worked intentionally to address basic needs like food, housing, transportation and financial stability. But when we ask students to leave their community to pursue a bachelor’s degree, we ask them to take on new burdens — higher costs, longer commutes and disrupted support systems. For many, that is where the journey stops.

If we are serious about basic needs, we must design pathways that allow students to continue their education without upending their lives.

We celebrate enrollment, but we must be equally committed to completion. Students are stopping out not because they lack ability, but because the pathway ends too soon or becomes too difficult to navigate. When universities are far away, schedules misaligned with working adults, and costs rising, the pathway becomes theoretical instead of practical.

Offering applied bachelor’s degrees at community colleges is not about expanding mission. It is about strengthening completion — ensuring that enrollment leads to outcomes.

Community colleges are deeply connected to the regions they serve. We work alongside local employers, understand workforce needs, and respond in real time. This is why the bachelor’s degrees already offered by community colleges have been successful: they are targeted, workforce-aligned, and built for the communities they serve.

This is not duplication. This is responsiveness.

Programs offered far away or only online do not meet the same need as locally grounded, applied programs designed for place-bound students. Engagement means meeting people where they are—not asking them to leave to succeed.

California’s Master Plan for Higher Education was visionary, but it was built for a different time. Today’s students are older, more likely to be working, and rooted in their communities. Housing costs are higher. Life is more complex.

We cannot rely on a system that assumes students can relocate, pause their lives, or absorb rising costs. We must build one that reflects how people actually live.

This is not an argument against universities. California needs strong UC and CSU systems. This is an argument for alignment—recognizing that community colleges are already doing this work and doing it well and giving institutions like College of the Desert the ability to respond to the needs we see every day.

Most importantly, this is about our students.

Our responsibility is not simply to open doors. It is to walk alongside students until they reach the finish line. They do not get there alone.

We rise together.

The question is no longer whether community colleges should offer bachelor’s degrees. The question is whether we are ready to fully deliver on the promise we have made.

The moment is here.

Val Martinez Garcia, who first joined College of the Desert in early 2023 as vice president of instruction, was appointed acting superintendent in 2024 and superintendent in 2025.

title: Change to Palm Springs mayor elections would roll back progress
author: em
raw_excerpt: In 2018, Palm Springs changed its election system because it had to.
body_text:
In 2018, Palm Springs changed its election system because it had to.

At the time, Latinos made up roughly a quarter of the city’s population — yet every single member of the city council was white. Not one Latino held a seat. The city faced a potential California Voting Rights Act lawsuit because the lack of representation was not accidental; it was structural.

District elections were adopted to correct that imbalance. And they worked.

Since moving to districts, for the first time in our city’s history, Latina women have risen to the highest levels of local leadership. Grace Garner became the first Latina mayor of Palm Springs in 2022. Today, Naomi Soto currently serves as mayor after rotating into the position in December 2025.

These milestones did not happen by chance. They happened because the system changed. Before district elections, Palm Springs had never elected a Latina woman to the city council or as mayor.

That is not the distant past. That is modern Palm Springs.

Now, the city is considering a change: keeping the five council districts in place, but adding a directly elected, citywide mayor.

On its face, that may sound like a minor adjustment. It is not.

Palm Springs has never elected a Latino or Hispanic candidate to the office of mayor in a citywide election — not once in its history. The only reason the city has had Latina mayors at all is because of the rotation system made possible under district elections.

Citywide elections historically favor candidates with established fundraising networks, broad name recognition and access to traditional power structures. When analysis by National Demographics Corporation of the 2007, 2013, 2015 and 2017 mayoral and council elections found signs of racially polarized voting between Latino and white voters, it exposed a reality many already knew: at-large systems can dilute the political power of communities of color.

District elections disrupted that imbalance. Returning to a citywide mayor risks restoring it.

Palm Springs proudly brands itself as progressive and inclusive. But inclusion is not measured by marketing slogans or symbolic gestures. It is measured by who holds power — and who has a fair chance to win it.

Rolling back structural reforms that finally opened the door to broader representation sends a clear message: that as communities of color begin to gain real influence, there is a push to change the rules again.

That is not progress. That is regression.

If we believe in equity, we must defend the systems that make equity possible. Democracy is not just about voting — it is about designing institutions that reflect the people who live here. Palm Springs cannot claim to champion justice while dismantling the very reforms that brought it closer to being representative.

The question before us is clear: Will we protect the gains made in 2018, or will we retreat to a system that historically excluded large portions of our community?

Progress should not be reversible simply because it shifts who holds power.

Deiter Crawford lives in Desert Highland Gateway Estates in Palm Springs.
